sections.directions: Directions: Each set of questions in this section is based on a single passage. The questions are to be answered on the basis of what is stated or implied in the passage. For some questions, more than one of the choices could conceivably answer the question. However, you are to choose the best answer; that is, choose the response that most accurately and completely answers the question.

items.stimulusText:
For centuries trial by jury has been a fundamental right in many democratic societies. However, in Canada and the United States, members of the military are instead subject to prosecution under a court-martial system if and when the accused's charges so warrant. A general court-martial is convened to deal with serious breaches of military discipline and it shares many characteristics with a criminal jury trial. It is made up of a prosecutor, defense counsel, judge, and an independent panel that decides whether the accused has been proved guilty. This panel functions like a civilian jury, but with important differences. In the United States, a two-thirds majority of a panel of no fewer than five members is required for conviction in most courts-martial, and in Canada a simple majority of a five-member panel is required.

In Canada, verdicts in civilian criminal trials have always been required to be unanimous. And although the U.S. Supreme Court has held that convictions based on nine guilty votes in a twelve-member jury do not violate the principle of proof of guilt beyond a reasonable doubt, there was some clear discomfort with the decision. One dissenting judge cited a study showing that in 10 percent of cases in the United States decided by juries whose verdicts were required to be unanimous, an initial minority eventually prevailed. Another judge, while voting with the majority, cautioned that a conviction based on a vote of seven to five would face much greater opposition from the court.

The supreme courts of both countries have expressed strong opinions on how the number of jurors affects the rights of the accused. In one case, the U.S. Supreme Court ruled unanimously that a conviction by five out of six jurors violated the accused's right to a fair trial, on the grounds that in such small juries, the need for unanimity is more compelling. The Canadian Supreme Court, citing studies showing that larger juries spend more time deliberating, explore more factual issues, and are more likely to remember essential information, found that six-member juries in two of the provinces were unfair to people tried in those provinces.

Because of the smaller size of court-martial panels and their lack of a unanimity requirement, it would seem that the rights of defendants have greater protection in civilian courts. The argument that allowing hung juries in courts-martial would impair the efficiency of the military is well countered by a study that found that no more than 1 percent of all trials in Canada result in hung juries. And the benefits of larger juries clearly outweigh the additional costs. The military judicial systems in these countries should be reformed so that, at least in times of peace, they may more closely resemble the parallel systems of civilian courts.
items.stemText: Which one of the following most accurately expresses the main point of the passage?

options.optionContent: The Canadian and U.S. militaries will have to change their rules of court procedure in order to avoid pressure resulting from recent supreme court decisions.
Due to structural flaws, military courts in North America are far more likely to convict the innocent than are civilian criminal courts.
The military's argument that the demanding conditions under which it operates compel it to limit defendants' rights does not hold up under scrutiny.
The rights of military defendants would be afforded greater protection in Canada and the United States by making the rules for court-martial panels more like those for civilian juries.
Large jury size and the requirement of unanimous verdicts generally make civilian courts more favorable to the accused than are courts-martial.

items.stemText: The passage states that the U.S. Supreme Court has ruled that

options.optionContent: majority verdicts in large juries never violate the rights of the accused
nonunanimous convictions in six-member juries are unfair
a two-thirds majority is required to convict in courts-martial
six-member juries are too small to weigh arguments and evidence fairly
military courts are not required to reach unanimous verdicts

items.stemText: The passage implies that which one of the following is an advantage of requiring that verdicts be unanimous?

options.optionContent: Requiring unanimity gives jurors more confidence in the eventual verdict.
Convictions based on unanimous decisions are less likely to be overruled than convictions based on majority decisions.
Juries whose verdicts are required to be unanimous acquit more often than do majority panels.
Requiring unanimity allows juries to be smaller.
Requiring unanimity provides a greater opportunity for a minority opinion to be considered.

items.stemText: The author discusses the Canadian Supreme Court's decision pertaining to small juries (last sentence of the third paragraph) primarily in order to

options.optionContent: show that while civilian jurisprudence is generally preferable to courts-martial, there are, nevertheless, cases in which defendants may not receive fair trials
show that rules about jury size are more consistent in Canada than in the U.S.
suggest that if such small juries violate defendants' rights in civilian trials, then small juries in courts-martial probably discriminate in the same way
contrast this decision with a similar decision of the U.S. Supreme Court
contrast the sizes of juries in the U.S. and Canada

items.stemText: Which one of the following most accurately describes the function of the final paragraph?

options.optionContent: to present the main conclusion of the passage while rebutting a possible objection
to show how the evidence presented in the second and third paragraphs is related to the main conclusion of the passage
to consider a possible objection to the conclusions of the second and third paragraphs
to modify the claims of the first paragraph in the light of evidence from the second and third paragraphs
to uncover flaws in an argument developed in the first three paragraphs and show how they can be avoided

items.stemText: It can be inferred from the passage that the author would most likely agree with which one of the following statements?

options.optionContent: Increasing the size of military juries would make courts-martial as fair to the accused as civilian trials.
Because of its unique institutions, it is unlikely that the military will accept court reform in the near future.
Some of the differences between courts-martial and civilian trials may be more justified in wartime.
The number of jurors in military courts should be increased to nine.
A hung jury is more likely when the jury is small than when it is large.

items.stemText: The reasoning in which one of the following arguments is most closely analogous to the counterargument that relies on the percentage of hung juries in Canada (second sentence of the last paragraph)?

options.optionContent: By issuing a warranty for their car's computer system, the manufacturer risks a considerable expense for replacing each broken system. However, since so few systems break, the company can afford the replacement costs.
Traffic accidents can be fatal. But since very few automobile accidents occur at this intersection, one need not be attentive when driving here.

options.optionLetter: Admission costs for our symphony's performances should not be raised. Such an action would result in many fewer tickets sold and result in overall lower revenue.
options.optionContent: Admission costs for our symphony's performances should not be raised. Such an action would result in many fewer tickets sold and result in overall lower revenue.

Measuring ingredients carefully when preparing to bake tends to increase the time required to make bread. Nevertheless, because bread does not rise if the ingredients are not measured carefully, all bakers must take the extra time to measure carefully.

Since most students who maintain part-time employment also find enough time to devote to their studies, it is likely that, if Jerri works part time while going to school, she will find time for her studies.

items.stimulusText:
Passage A is from a review of author Eudora Welty's collected writings; passage B is from the introduction to a collection of Welty's photographs.

Passage A

"I am a writer," Eudora Welty writes in her memoir, "who came of a sheltered life. A sheltered life can be a daring life as well. For all serious daring starts from within." Her choice of the word "daring" is an interesting one, for, in retrospect, it is daring, above all, that turns out to have been the outstanding trait of this quiet Mississippi woman whose extra-literary life was, in the normal sense of the words, neither daring nor adventurous. Born in 1909 in Jackson, Mississippi, the much-loved eldest child of an affectionate, cultivated, middle-class family, she lived, except for a few years at the University of Wisconsin and at the Columbia University Business School, in Mississippi all her life. When she died at age 92, she still resided in the family home built by her father in the 1920s.

But in the practice of her art, Welty was as stylistically daring as any American of the twentieth century. Possibly more so, for even the great innovators like Hemingway and Faulkner, once they found their distinctive voices, refined and developed them rather than sailing off again into entirely uncharted territory, as Welty did. Having perfected an entirely individual style of verbal farce with her early stories "Petrified Man" and "Why I Live at the P.O.," she was simultaneously producing tales in a manner that can loosely be called Southern Gothic. As she matured, she turned away from both genres in favor of a complex, layered style that allowed her to synthesize her many gifts.

Passage B

In the twenty-odd years that the camera demanded a piece of her time, Eudora Welty employed with unmistakable intensity the same acuity and patience that distinguishes her fiction. Try to imagine these pictures coming from any other writer of the time—Cather, Porter, Faulkner, Hemingway, Steinbeck: all unthinkable, in various interesting ways. In all of her writings Welty chose or accepted the role of intent observer, unsparing but benign. And behind the lens, in those early days when her hand was only beginning to write, her eye was just as keen and steady and—if possible—even more merciful, as though the breathing fact of a person who stood before her was a solemn trust not to be betrayed.

Other photographers, with more sophisticated equipment and greater technical proficiency, were working nearby, photographing similar subjects, but toward different ends. In Walker Evans's pictures from Alabama, the faces of poor families burn with a bitter adamance—or are stunned and frozen—in a way not seen in Welty's pictures. And Dorothea Lange's eloquent photographs show forever those dispossessed Southern farmers who stand, if only for that one instant, facing their fate in a baffled pain too naked to watch without some futile longing to help them. But no one from that time had a broader range of vision than Welty.
items.stemText: Which one of the following statements about the authors of the two passages can be inferred from the passages?

options.optionContent: One of them is mainly a writer of fiction, whereas the other is mainly a writer of nonfiction.
One of them has interviewed or otherwise conversed directly with Welty, whereas the other has not.
Both of them believe themselves to be knowledgeable about Welty's fiction.
Both of them consider themselves to be competent critics of Welty's nonfiction.
Both of them have at least some experience in analyzing and critiquing the art of photography.

items.stemText: The authors' attitudes toward Welty's literary work can most accurately be described in which one of the following ways?

options.optionContent: Both authors have admiration for it.
Both authors view it in an analytical way without any value judgment.
The author of passage A has a highly favorable attitude toward it, whereas the author of passage B is neutral about it.
The author of passage A has a highly favorable attitude toward it, whereas the author of passage B has a mildly negative attitude toward some of it.
The author of passage A is ambivalent toward it, whereas the author of passage B is mildly appreciative of it.

items.stemText: The primary function of the first paragraph of passage B is to

options.optionContent: indicate aspects of Welty's approach to photography that are shared with her approach to writing
compare Welty's photography to that of professional photographers of the time
interpret Welty's writing and photography by placing them in the context of Southern culture
interpret Welty's photography by relating it to events from her life
contrast Welty's photography with that of the photography of other writers who also engaged in photography

Possibly more so, for even the great innovators like Hemingway and Faulkner, once they found their distinctive voices, refined and developed them rather than sailing off again into entirely uncharted territory, as Welty did. Having perfected an entirely individual style of verbal farce with her early stories "Petrified Man" and "Why I Live at the P.O.," she was simultaneously producing tales in a manner that can loosely be called Southern Gothic. As she matured, she turned away from both genres in favor of a complex, layered style that allowed her to synthesize her many gifts.

Passage B

In the twenty-odd years that the camera demanded a piece of her time, Eudora Welty employed with unmistakable intensity the same acuity and patience that distinguishes her fiction. Try to imagine these pictures coming from any other writer of the time—Cather, Porter, Faulkner, Hemingway, Steinbeck: all unthinkable, in various interesting ways. In all of her writings Welty chose or accepted the role of intent observer, unsparing but benign. And behind the lens, in those early days when her hand was only beginning to write, her eye was just as keen and steady and—if possible—even more merciful, as though the breathing fact of a person who stood before her was a solemn trust not to be betrayed.

Other photographers, with more sophisticated equipment and greater technical proficiency, were working nearby, photographing similar subjects, but toward different ends. In Walker Evans's pictures from Alabama, the faces of poor families burn with a bitter adamance—or are stunned and frozen—in a way not seen in Welty's pictures. And Dorothea Lange's eloquent photographs show forever those dispossessed Southern farmers who stand, if only for that one instant, facing their fate in a baffled pain too naked to watch without some futile longing to help them. But no one from that time had a broader range of vision than Welty.
items.stemText: Which one of the following is an approach used in both passages to help make a point about Welty's artistic work?

options.optionContent: giving a detailed account of Welty's early life
surveying critical reaction to particular works by Welty
celebrating Welty's artistic achievements by citing specific examples that embody those achievements
comparing Welty's creative work with that of others working in the same artistic medium or genre
highlighting aspects of Welty's art that deal with themes that are particularly characteristic of the South

items.stimulusText:
Passage A is from a review of author Eudora Welty's collected writings; passage B is from the introduction to a collection of Welty's photographs.

Passage A

"I am a writer," Eudora Welty writes in her memoir, "who came of a sheltered life. A sheltered life can be a daring life as well. For all serious daring starts from within." Her choice of the word "daring" is an interesting one, for, in retrospect, it is daring, above all, that turns out to have been the outstanding trait of this quiet Mississippi woman whose extra-literary life was, in the normal sense of the words, neither daring nor adventurous. Born in 1909 in Jackson, Mississippi, the much-loved eldest child of an affectionate, cultivated, middle-class family, she lived, except for a few years at the University of Wisconsin and at the Columbia University Business School, in Mississippi all her life. When she died at age 92, she still resided in the family home built by her father in the 1920s.

But in the practice of her art, Welty was as stylistically daring as any American of the twentieth century. Possibly more so, for even the great innovators like Hemingway and Faulkner, once they found their distinctive voices, refined and developed them rather than sailing off again into entirely uncharted territory, as Welty did. Having perfected an entirely individual style of verbal farce with her early stories "Petrified Man" and "Why I Live at the P.O.," she was simultaneously producing tales in a manner that can loosely be called Southern Gothic. As she matured, she turned away from both genres in favor of a complex, layered style that allowed her to synthesize her many gifts.

Passage B

In the twenty-odd years that the camera demanded a piece of her time, Eudora Welty employed with unmistakable intensity the same acuity and patience that distinguishes her fiction. Try to imagine these pictures coming from any other writer of the time—Cather, Porter, Faulkner, Hemingway, Steinbeck: all unthinkable, in various interesting ways. In all of her writings Welty chose or accepted the role of intent observer, unsparing but benign. And behind the lens, in those early days when her hand was only beginning to write, her eye was just as keen and steady and—if possible—even more merciful, as though the breathing fact of a person who stood before her was a solemn trust not to be betrayed.

Other photographers, with more sophisticated equipment and greater technical proficiency, were working nearby, photographing similar subjects, but toward different ends. In Walker Evans's pictures from Alabama, the faces of poor families burn with a bitter adamance—or are stunned and frozen—in a way not seen in Welty's pictures. And Dorothea Lange's eloquent photographs show forever those dispossessed Southern farmers who stand, if only for that one instant, facing their fate in a baffled pain too naked to watch without some futile longing to help them. But no one from that time had a broader range of vision than Welty.
items.stemText: The relationship between passage A and passage B is most analogous to the relationship between the two television programs described in which one of the following?

options.optionContent: A television program about architecture is narrated by a famous architect; another program discusses ways in which that architect's building designs were influenced by his knowledge of other art forms.
A television program discusses an architect's collaboration with engineers and urban planners; another program compares that architect's building designs with those of several other architects.

A television program presents the contrasting views of several scholars regarding an architect's work; another program discusses the ways in which a number of architects have reacted to the views of one of those critics.

A television program discusses an architect's early development and education; another program analyzes the similarities among a number of superficially different building designs that that architect produced.
A television program examines the relationship between an architect's personal experiences and his building designs; another program compares that architect's sculptures to his building designs.

items.stemText: The primary purpose of passage A is to

options.optionContent: contrast the literary work of an author with that of her contemporaries
support the claim that a particular author's work was stylistically daring
describe the literary genres in which a particular author wrote
defend the judgment that a particular author's later work is superior to her earlier work
provide evidence that a particular author's work was compassionate

description: The Drill Set contains reading comprehension question types. Each question involves comprehending and analyzing lengthy and complex materials, encompassing both single and paired passages.
